## What a large language model is

In `GRA 4164`, a large language model is best understood as a very large neural
network trained to predict the next token in a sequence. That sounds narrow, but it
is enough to produce surprisingly broad behaviour: text generation, summarization,
question answering, translation, coding assistance, and many other language tasks.

The core pattern is the same one students already saw in simpler form with n-gram
models: use previous context to predict what comes next. The difference is scale,
representation quality, and the transformer architecture used to model long-range
dependencies.

## Why LLMs became useful

Three ingredients came together:

- much larger text corpora,
- much more compute,
- and model architectures that scale effectively.

Training compresses large amounts of text into model weights. Inference then uses
those weights to produce a probability distribution over possible next tokens.

This does not mean the model stores explicit rules or a database of facts in a
human-readable way. It means statistical regularities from the training data are
encoded in the parameters of the network.

## LLMs still learn through self-supervision

The training signal is built from the text itself. If a sequence reads:

`We know what comes next in this [sentence]`

then the next token in the original text is the supervision target. This is why
LLM training is called `self-supervised`: the corpus supplies both the input and
the prediction target.

At a high level, the lifecycle usually includes:

1. pre-training on large unlabeled text corpora,
2. supervised fine-tuning on curated instruction-response pairs,
3. additional alignment stages to improve usefulness and reduce harmful behaviour.

Lecture 7 only needs the overview. The architecture details come later.

## Tokens are not the same as words

A model does not read raw text directly. It reads `tokens`, which are discrete units
from a learned vocabulary. Depending on the tokenizer, a token may be:

- a whole word,
- part of a word,
- punctuation,
- whitespace,
- or a chunk of bytes.

This matters because many LLM behaviours that look mysterious are really token-level
effects. If a word is split into awkward subparts, spelling can be harder. If a
number is broken into several tokens, arithmetic can become less stable. If source
code is tokenized poorly, programming performance suffers.

## Why tokenization exists

A model needs a finite vocabulary. Word-level vocabularies become too large and
cannot handle unseen terms well. Character-level vocabularies are flexible but make
sequences much longer. Modern tokenizers compromise by using subword units.

That compromise matters operationally:

- shorter sequences are cheaper to process,
- rare words can still be represented through smaller pieces,
- and multilingual text can share useful fragments across vocabularies.

## Unicode and UTF-8 come first

Before tokenization, text is just encoded characters. Unicode gives each character a
code point, while UTF-8 maps characters to one or more bytes.

That is important because modern tokenizers often begin from byte-compatible text
representations. The same visible string can therefore be thought of at several
levels:

1. characters,
2. bytes,
3. tokens,
4. model embeddings.

Understanding that stack helps explain why emojis, accented characters, and
non-English scripts can behave differently from plain ASCII text.

## Byte-pair encoding explains modern subword tokenizers

One common approach is Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE). The logic is simple:

1. start with small units,
2. count frequent adjacent pairs,
3. merge the most common pair,
4. repeat until the vocabulary reaches the desired size.

This creates subword units that are more flexible than whole words and more compact
than character-only tokenization. Common fragments become single tokens, while rare
or unseen words can still be decomposed into smaller pieces.

The outcome is practical rather than linguistically perfect. Tokenizers are built to
support model efficiency and coverage, not to reflect the "true" structure of human
language.

## Why tokenization affects model behaviour

The legacy tokenizer notebook highlights four recurring consequences:

- spelling can be unreliable because the model predicts tokens, not letters,
- arithmetic can be weak because numbers are split into token sequences rather than
  treated like exact symbolic objects,
- non-English text may be represented less efficiently if the tokenizer is biased
  toward English-heavy corpora,
- and code performance depends heavily on whether common programming patterns are
  tokenized cleanly.

::: {.teacher-only}
A useful classroom prompt is to ask students why an LLM can write a convincing
paragraph about arithmetic but still make small calculation errors. The right answer
starts with representation, not intelligence.
:::

## From static embeddings to contextual tokens

Word2Vec gave one vector per word type. LLMs go further by producing contextual
representations for tokens inside a sequence. The token `bank` in a sentence about
finance does not need to be represented the same way as `bank` in a sentence about a
river.

That is a major conceptual shift from classical word embeddings to transformer-based
language models, and it is why tokenization is not just preprocessing. It shapes the
units from which all later contextual reasoning is built.

## What to report

When students explain LLM tokenization in this course, they should report:

- what a token is and why it is not always a word,
- why subword tokenization is a practical compromise,
- how Unicode/UTF-8 and tokenization relate,
- one concrete way tokenization affects model performance, and
- one limitation of token-based language modelling that remains visible in practice.
